content.why_exists: Data persistence and organization. You need to move, copy, and delete data as your project evolves.
content.problem: If you copy a 1GB file, why does it take a long time? If you move that same file on the same disk, why is it instant? Understanding the difference is key to system performance.
content.beginner_explanation: Copying ('cp') creates a completely new file with the same data. Moving ('mv') just changes the 'name tag' pointing to the data, unless you move it to a different disk.
content.analogy: Copying is like printing a second copy of a document. Moving is like taking the original document and putting it in a different folder—the paper itself doesn't change, just its location.
content.kernel_story: Every file is an 'Inode'. When you 'mv' a file on the same partition, the kernel just updates the directory entry to point to the same Inode number. No data is moved!
content.execution_flow: 1. mv source target. 2. Kernel verifies source exists. 3. Kernel creates link in target directory. 4. Kernel removes link in source directory.
content.visual_model: Two directory folders pointing to the same 'Data Block' (Inode) after a move operation.
content.formal_definition: Fundamental operations for managing filesystem objects, categorized by their interaction with Inode pointers and physical data blocks.
content.kernel_internals: The 'unlink()' syscall is what actually 'deletes' a file by reducing its link count. If the count reaches zero, the kernel marks the data blocks as free.
content.data_flow: Userspace -> rename() syscall -> VFS -> Filesystem Driver -> Disk Controller.
content.performance_implications: Moving across mount points (different disks) forces a slow copy+delete operation. Always check if you are crossing filesystem boundaries.
content.security_implications: Removing a file ('rm') doesn't actually erase the data; it just removes the pointer. Forensic tools can recover 'deleted' data unless it is overwritten.
content.common_mistakes: Running 'rm -rf /' as root. This is the most famous mistake in Linux history and will destroy your entire OS.
content.real_world_workflows: Using 'rsync' instead of 'cp' for large data transfers to handle network interruptions and only copy changed parts.
content.professional_usage: Sysadmins use 'alias rm="rm -i"' to force a confirmation prompt before every deletion to prevent accidents.

mini_labs.task: Create a directory, move a file into it, and then delete the whole thing.
mini_labs.command: mkdir backup; touch notes.txt; mv notes.txt backup/; rm -rf backup

content.troubleshooting: If 'rm' fails, check if the file is 'immutable' using 'lsattr'. Even root can't delete an immutable file!
interview_questions: What is the difference between a hard link and a soft link?
How does the kernel handle a file that is deleted while still being used by a process?
content.mini_recap: Moving is about pointers; copying is about data. Understanding Inodes makes you a faster and safer engineer.
content.next_topic: Now that we can move files, let's learn how to look inside them without opening a heavy editor: Text Processing.
